title: Adam Smith
summary: Scottish moral philosopher whose Wealth of Nations (1776) provided the foundational analysis of market economies, the division of labor, and the case for free trade — and whose Theory of Moral Sentiments (1759) grounded economic behavior in a broader account of human sympathy and ethics.
hook: Adam Smith is simultaneously the most cited and most misread thinker in economics. The man who supposedly said greed is good spent most of his career writing about sympathy, justice, and the moral foundations of social life. Getting Smith right matters because getting him wrong has consequences.

beginner.theFigure:
Adam Smith gave the world an early full explanation of how a market economy organizes itself without anyone in charge. Prices, wages, and supply all adjust through countless separate private decisions, with no planner required. That single idea helped make modern economics possible. He wrote two major books that changed how people understood money, trade, and fairness.

But most people only know half of Smith. They know he believed in the power of markets. They do not know that he also worried deeply about workers being treated badly. He worried about big companies cheating customers. He worried about governments helping the rich at the cost of the poor. He thought business owners were often a threat to the public good. He wanted workers to get a fair deal. He supported public schools paid for by the government. The real Adam Smith had far more to say than just "let markets run free."
beginner.worldOfIdeas:
In Smith's time, governments controlled trade very tightly. They gave special licenses to certain companies. They blocked foreign goods from coming in. They believed a country got rich by selling a lot abroad and buying as little as possible from other countries. This whole system was called mercantilism.

Smith thought this was wrong. He watched how real businesses actually worked. He noticed that when workers each did one small task very well, they made far more products than when each worker did every step alone. He described a pin factory where ten workers did different jobs. Those workers could make 48,000 pins a day together. Ten workers each making whole pins from start to finish could produce only about 200. That is a huge difference. Smith saw that breaking work into steps was central to making more things. He called this the division of labor. He treated it as the main engine of economic growth.
beginner.centralIdea:
Smith's biggest idea was that markets can organize themselves without anyone being in charge. Here is how it works. When bread gets scarce, its price goes up. Higher prices mean more profit for bakers. More bakers enter the market. Soon there is enough bread again and prices fall. No government had to plan any of this. Smith called this kind of quiet coordination the "invisible hand."

But Smith also knew that markets could fail. Business owners often tried to work together secretly to keep prices high. Without real competition, the invisible hand stopped working. Smith also warned about what happens when workers do the same tiny task all day, every day. Doing one small job forever, he wrote, made workers "as stupid and ignorant as it is possible for a human creature to become." He said the state had a duty to pay for schools so workers could stay sharp and informed. Markets needed support to work well for everyone.
beginner.howSpread:
Smith's ideas spread fast after his death, but people often took only the parts they liked best and found most useful. His book about trade became a guide for people who wanted fewer rules on buying and selling. His arguments helped tear apart the old system that restricted trade.

His other book, the one about sympathy and moral life, was mostly forgotten by economists. Over time, the invisible hand came to stand for nearly all of Smith's message. Many of his warnings about powerful companies and poorly paid workers were quietly dropped. In the twentieth century, some governments used Smith's name to argue for cutting taxes and removing business rules. They left out the parts where he worried about inequality. They ignored his calls for public schools, public roads, and rules for banks.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
Very different groups all claim Adam Smith as their own hero. People who want fewer government rules point to his belief in free markets. People who worry about inequality point to his warnings about powerful companies and badly paid workers. Philosophers point to his book on morals. They argue that you cannot understand his economics without reading that book too.

There is also a real question about how far his ideas can reach. Smith was writing before giant factories and global companies existed. He was watching small workshops and local markets in Scotland. Whether his ideas truly fit our world of huge banks and global supply chains is something scholars still argue about. Smith himself knew that free trade arguments could be used by powerful countries to protect their own interests at others' expense. He was cautious about that. What is clear is that the version of Smith people invoke in most debates captures only part of what he actually believed and wrote.

general.theFigure:
The ideas Adam Smith made possible still frame almost every argument about free trade, taxation, and the role of government. His two major works gave economics much of its founding vocabulary. What makes him genuinely interesting is the gap between what he actually argued and what his legacy became.

The phrase "invisible hand" appears only three times across his published writings. In each case, he used it narrowly, not as a general law about market efficiency. Yet it became the shorthand for an entire ideology. The market liberals who claimed his authority were not simply inventing things. The Wealth of Nations does argue that competitive markets tend to produce good outcomes without central planning. But they selected one strand of a much richer analysis. They set aside his deep hostility to monopoly, his worry about workers ground down by dull labor, and his insistence that markets rest on moral and legal foundations they cannot produce by themselves. To read Smith whole is to find someone who trusted competitive markets but did not trust merchants.
general.worldOfIdeas:
Smith emerged from the Scottish Enlightenment, a network of thinkers who believed human social life could be understood through careful observation and reason. His teacher Francis Hutcheson had grounded morality in benevolence. His friend David Hume was rewriting moral philosophy at the same time. These thinkers shared a commitment to studying human institutions and moral habits as facts about real people.

The economic world Smith inhabited ran on mercantilism. This was the doctrine that a nation's wealth lay in its hoard of gold and silver, that exports were good, and that imports were harmful. Governments managed trade accordingly. Monopoly charters went to favored companies. Guilds blocked entry to trades. Smith saw this system as wasteful and morally corrupt, a machine built by merchants and manufacturers to extract advantage from consumers and workers.

One of his intellectual targets was Bernard Mandeville, who argued in The Fable of the Bees that private vices produce public benefits, since the desires of the rich employ the poor. Smith accepted one part of this. Self-interested behavior in markets could produce useful social outcomes. But he rejected Mandeville's moral nihilism. His key move was showing that this result required real institutional conditions: genuine competition, legal order, and a moral culture that Mandeville had simply taken for granted.
general.centralIdea:
The central observation of the Wealth of Nations is simple. Specialization increases productivity. Smith's pin factory showed this clearly. Ten men performing distinct tasks could produce 48,000 pins a day. The same ten men, each making whole pins alone, could manage perhaps 200. Dividing labor was the engine of economic growth. Specialization required exchange, exchange required markets, and extending markets required trade. The argument for free trade followed from this logic.

But Smith's view of markets was more cautious than many of his admirers acknowledged. He noted that workers usually bargained at a disadvantage, since they needed wages to survive while employers could afford to wait. He saw that businessmen tended to collude: "People of the same trade seldom meet together, even for merriment and diversion, but the conversation ends in a conspiracy against the public." He supported banking rules and public provision of schools and infrastructure.

The Theory of Moral Sentiments supplied the moral foundation for all this. Smith argued that people form moral judgments by imagining how a fair, impartial observer would view their conduct. We build this inner judge through social experience and through the imaginative power he called sympathy. It is what shapes and bounds self-interest. Markets work partly because the people in them have been morally formed. When that formation breaks down, markets tend to break down too.
general.howSpread:
In his own time, Smith was read as a political economist in the full sense: someone analyzing the conditions under which nations prosper, with a critical edge aimed at monopolists and the political corruption they cultivated. The economists who followed him engaged with much of this. Even so, Ricardo's formalization of Smith began stripping away the moral philosophy early in the nineteenth century.

The decisive shift came as economics professionalized. Moral philosophy was set aside as belonging to a different subject. Historical and institutional analysis gave way to abstract models. What remained was a stylized picture of self-interested actors in competitive markets. Smith's authority was invoked for this picture despite its distance from what he had actually written.

In the twentieth century, the Cold War gave this process an ideological charge. Market liberalism wanted a founding father, and a simplified Smith served the purpose. Political movements justified deregulation in his name, ignoring that Smith had supported progressive taxation, public schools, and banking oversight. He had viewed concentrated business power as a standing threat to the public. Since the late twentieth century, scholars such as Emma Rothschild and Amartya Sen have worked to recover the fuller Smith from beneath this inheritance.
general.contestedInheritance:
Three readings of Smith organize the ongoing debate, each drawing on different parts of his work.

The market liberal reading, dominant in economics and policy circles, treats the Wealth of Nations as a demonstration that competitive markets coordinate self-interested behavior to produce beneficial outcomes. Free trade and limited government follow as policy conclusions. This reading has real textual support. But it requires treating large portions of Smith's writing as peripheral. It also turns a thinker who was deeply suspicious of business power into a champion of business interests.

The moral economy reading emphasizes the Theory of Moral Sentiments and argues that Smith's economics cannot be understood without its moral foundation. On this account, markets are social institutions that depend on norms of sympathy and fairness. Their capacity to function well rests on the moral development of the people who participate in them. This reading is associated above all with Amartya Sen, whose capabilities approach to human development draws explicitly on it.

The historical reading insists on placing Smith in his specific context: the debates about commercial society and international rivalry in eighteenth-century Europe. On this reading, his arguments were responses to particular historical problems, not timeless theorems. The case for free trade was made against specific distortions in mercantilist Britain. It was not meant as a universal prescription for all times and places. This reading, developed by scholars including Istvan Hont and Donald Winch, limits how directly Smith can be applied to current debates, while clarifying what he actually meant.

advanced.theFigure:
The historiographical problem with Adam Smith is the problem of canonical distortion: a thinker's actual views become progressively less visible as his authority becomes progressively more important. The Smith who appears in economics textbooks and policy debates is a construction assembled from selected passages, stripped of their historical and philosophical context, and made to serve purposes Smith did not intend. Recovering the actual Smith requires archaeological work — reading him against his sources, his contemporaries, and the specific intellectual and political debates he was engaging.

The most important recent contribution to this recovery is the scholarship of Istvan Hont, whose Jealousy of Trade (2005) situates Smith in the long tradition of European debate about commercial society and international competition. Hont's Smith is not primarily a theorist of market efficiency but a theorist of the political conditions under which commercial society is possible — the legal and institutional framework that makes markets function, the international order that makes trade possible, and the political economy of the nation-state in a world of commercial competition. This Smith is far more attentive to the conditions and limits of markets than the ideological Smith invoked in policy debates.
advanced.worldOfIdeas:
Smith's engagement with Mandeville is the crucial intellectual moment for understanding his mature position. Bernard Mandeville's Fable of the Bees (1714) had argued that private vices — selfishness, vanity, luxury — produce public benefits: the vicious desires of the rich create employment for artisans and merchants, and the pursuit of self-interest drives economic activity that benefits all. Mandeville was deliberately provocative, but his argument had a serious core that required serious response.

Smith's response was to accept part of Mandeville's insight — that market exchange could coordinate self-interested behavior to produce socially beneficial outcomes — while rejecting his moral psychology. For Smith, the relevant motive in market exchange was not vice but what he called 'the desire of bettering our condition' — a universal human drive that was morally neutral, not vicious. And the social outcomes of market exchange depended on institutional conditions — competitive markets, legal order, property rights — that Mandeville had ignored.

The Theory of Moral Sentiments provided the alternative moral psychology. Smith's account of sympathy — the imaginative capacity to enter into another's situation — was both a descriptive account of how moral judgments are formed and a normative standard against which conduct could be evaluated. The impartial spectator — the internalized perspective of a fair observer — provided the criterion for moral judgment that Mandeville's pure self-interest theory lacked. This moral psychology is not peripheral to Smith's economics; it is its foundation.
advanced.centralIdea:
The philosophical crux of Smith's system is the relationship between self-interest and sympathy — between the motivating force of market behavior and the moral framework within which it operates. The standard reading treats these as compartmentalized: the Theory of Moral Sentiments governs private moral life while the Wealth of Nations governs public economic life. This reading — sometimes called 'Das Adam Smith Problem' by German scholars who thought they detected a contradiction between the two works — has been largely abandoned by serious Smith scholarship, but it survives in popularized accounts.

The more sophisticated reading, developed by philosophers including Charles Griswold and Ryan Hanley, treats the two works as complementary analyses of the same human nature. The same capacity for sympathy that makes moral judgment possible also makes exchange possible — we can estimate what others value and what they will accept in trade because we can imaginatively enter their situation. The desire of bettering our condition that drives economic activity is channeled through social norms that sympathy produces and the impartial spectator enforces. Markets are not separate from morality but embedded in it.

This has a practical implication that Smith drew explicitly: markets require a background of trust, honesty, and legal order that they cannot themselves produce. The 'propensity to truck, barter, and exchange' that Smith identified as the basis of commercial society presupposes a framework of mutual recognition and reliable commitment that market mechanisms alone cannot sustain. This is the Smith who is relevant to contemporary debates about the institutional preconditions of market economies — and the Smith who is systematically absent from the ideological invocations of his authority.
advanced.howSpread:
The political economy of Smith's reception is itself a case study in the sociology of knowledge. The Smith who was received by Ricardo, Malthus, and the classical political economists was read in the context of the specific debates of early nineteenth-century Britain — about the Corn Laws, about poverty and population, about the wages of labor. Ricardo's transformation of Smith's labor theory of value into a more rigorous but narrower framework — dropping Smith's moral philosophy, his analysis of monopoly, and his concern for worker welfare to focus on prices, rent, and distribution as formal quantities — began the process of abstraction from Smith's historical and institutional concerns.

The marginalist revolution of the 1870s — Jevons, Menger, Walras — further transformed economic analysis by replacing the classical political economy's concern with growth, distribution, and historical change with a static analysis of individual choice and market equilibrium. Smith's authority was claimed for this new framework even though it represented a significant departure from his methods and concerns. The twentieth-century neoclassical synthesis built on this foundation, and the Smith who became its patron saint bore increasingly little resemblance to the historical Smith.

The Reagan-Thatcher invocation of Smith in the 1980s represented the ideological apex of this process. Free markets, deregulation, and the rolling back of the welfare state were justified by appeal to Smith's authority. That Smith had supported public education, public infrastructure, and progressive taxation; that he had viewed merchants and manufacturers as natural enemies of the public interest; that he had worried about the moral and intellectual degradation of workers under the division of labor — all of this was invisible in the ideological construction.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The most consequential contemporary debate about Smith concerns whether his framework can illuminate — or whether it systematically distorts — our understanding of contemporary capitalism. The critics argue that Smith was analyzing a specific historical moment — the emergence of commercial society in eighteenth-century Britain and Scotland — and that extending his analysis to twenty-first century financial capitalism, global supply chains, and platform monopolies requires assumptions he did not make and would not have endorsed.

Amartya Sen's use of Smith has been the most philosophically sophisticated attempt to recover a Smith relevant to contemporary concerns. Sen's Development as Freedom (1999) draws on Smith's moral philosophy — the Theory of Moral Sentiments' account of sympathy, the impartial spectator, and the conditions of human flourishing — to develop a capabilities approach to development that is explicitly critical of the narrowly economic metrics of GDP and growth. Sen's Smith is not a theorist of market efficiency but a theorist of the conditions under which human beings can live well — a framework that includes but is not reducible to material prosperity.

The political economy critique — associated with scholars like Ha-Joon Chang — argues that Smith's free trade case was made in the specific context of British commercial dominance, and that developing countries that followed Smith's prescriptions while Britain had industrialized under protection were following advice that served British interests rather than their own. Smith was quite aware of the conditions under which free trade arguments could be strategically deployed — he acknowledged that recommending free trade to a nation whose trading partners use protection may serve the stronger party's interests rather than universal welfare.

foundation.theStory: Adam Smith is one of those rare thinkers whose ideas became so influential that they were transformed in the process of being absorbed. The Smith who inhabits economics textbooks and political speeches is significantly different from the Smith who actually wrote two major works on moral philosophy and political economy in eighteenth-century Scotland. The gap between the historical Smith and the ideological Smith is itself a historical problem worth understanding.
foundation.theDebate: Historians and philosophers debate three main questions about Smith: the relationship between his two major works (are they compatible or in tension?); the degree to which his market analysis can be separated from its historical and institutional context; and whether his framework supports the free-market ideology that claims his authority or is fundamentally different from it. These are not merely academic debates — they have implications for how we understand markets, inequality, and the role of government.
foundation.whyItMatters: Smith matters because his analysis established the basic vocabulary and framework through which market economies are understood. Concepts like the division of labor, comparative advantage, the price system as a coordination mechanism, and the dangers of monopoly all trace back to Smith. But Smith also matters as a corrective to his own reputation: recovering what he actually argued — including his concerns about inequality, monopoly, and worker degradation — complicates the ideological uses to which his authority has been put.
questions: Smith argued that the division of labor increases productivity but also makes workers 'as stupid and ignorant as it is possible for a human creature to become.' How did he resolve this tension, and is his resolution satisfying?

Smith is claimed as the founding father of free-market economics, but he supported public education, public infrastructure, and regulation of banking. What does this tell us about how intellectual legacies are constructed?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The scholarly conversation on Smith divides between economists who read him primarily through the Wealth of Nations and as a precursor to neoclassical economics, and historians and philosophers who read him in his full intellectual context and find a much more complicated thinker. Key figures: Emma Rothschild's Economic Sentiments recovered Smith's radical and egalitarian dimensions; Amartya Sen's work connected Smith's moral philosophy to development economics; Istvan Hont's Jealousy of Trade situated Smith in European debates about commercial society and international competition; Nicholas Phillipson's biography reconstructed Smith's intellectual formation in the Scottish Enlightenment.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The primary evidence is Smith's own texts — the Theory of Moral Sentiments (1759), the Wealth of Nations (1776), and the posthumously published lectures and essays. The textual evidence is rich but requires careful contextualization: Smith was engaging with specific debates in his time, using terms that have shifted in meaning, and responding to thinkers (Mandeville, Hume, Hutcheson) whose work is less familiar to modern readers. The limit is that extending Smith's analysis to contemporary capitalism requires bridging a historical gap that his texts cannot entirely span.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Smith must engage with both major works and must situate him in his historical context rather than treating him as a timeless theorist. Arguments that invoke Smith's authority for contemporary free-market positions without engaging with his actual views on monopoly, worker welfare, and the moral foundations of markets are not serious scholarship. Arguments that dismiss Smith as simply an ideologist of capitalism without engaging with the sophistication of his moral philosophy are equally inadequate.
questions: Is the Theory of Moral Sentiments compatible with the Wealth of Nations, or does the former's emphasis on sympathy and moral development contradict the latter's analysis of self-interested market behavior?

Can Smith's free trade arguments — made in the context of eighteenth-century British commercial dominance — be applied to contemporary trade policy, or are they historically specific in ways that limit their contemporary relevance?

research.topic: The relationship between the Theory of Moral Sentiments and the Wealth of Nations
research.content: Scholarship since the 1970s has largely resolved the 'Das Adam Smith Problem' — the nineteenth-century German concern that the two works were in contradiction — by showing that they represent complementary analyses of human social life rather than contradictory accounts. The moral psychology of sympathy and the impartial spectator in the Theory of Moral Sentiments provides the foundation for understanding the social conditions under which market exchange is possible. Smith revised the Theory of Moral Sentiments six times, including a substantial revision in 1790, the year of his death — evidence that he continued to see it as a living and important work.

research.topic: Smith's actual policy positions versus his ideological reputation
research.content: Careful textual analysis has established that Smith's actual policy positions were significantly more nuanced than his free-market reputation suggests. He supported public provision of education, public infrastructure (roads, canals, harbors), progressive taxation, and regulation of banking and interest rates. He was deeply hostile to merchants and manufacturers as a class, viewing their political influence as a systematic threat to the public interest. He supported the navigation acts that restricted colonial trade when they served national defense. The Smith of policy debates is a selective construction.

research.topic: What Smith would have said about industrial capitalism
research.content: Smith died in 1790, before the industrial revolution was fully underway. The large factory system, the industrial working class, and the concentrated capital that would characterize nineteenth-century capitalism were only embryonically present in his experience. What evidence helps: Smith's analysis of the division of labor and its costs, his concerns about monopoly and concentrated economic power, and his moral philosophy's emphasis on the conditions of human flourishing all provide resources for thinking about how he might have responded. What must remain unknowable: whether Smith would have endorsed, modified, or rejected the extension of his framework to industrial capitalism, and what specific positions he would have taken on child labor, trade unions, or the factory system.

comparativeNarrative.name: Market Liberal Reading
comparativeNarrative.content: The dominant reading of Smith in economics and policy circles treats the Wealth of Nations as the founding text of free-market liberalism — the demonstration that market exchange, driven by self-interest and coordinated by prices, produces socially beneficial outcomes without central direction. On this reading, Smith's invisible hand is the key insight, free trade is the policy conclusion, and the Theory of Moral Sentiments is at most a peripheral work about personal ethics. This reading has been institutionally dominant — it is the Smith of economics textbooks, central bank speeches, and policy think tanks — and it has real textual support in the Wealth of Nations. Its limitation is that it requires ignoring large portions of what Smith actually wrote.

comparativeNarrative.name: Capability and Moral Economy Reading
comparativeNarrative.content: Amartya Sen's reading of Smith emphasizes the Theory of Moral Sentiments and its account of sympathy, the impartial spectator, and the conditions of human flourishing. For Sen, Smith is not primarily a theorist of market efficiency but a theorist of human development — of the conditions under which people can live well and exercise their capacities. This reading connects Smith to contemporary debates about inequality, human development, and the limits of GDP as a measure of welfare. It has the advantage of taking both of Smith's major works seriously but has been criticized for reading Smith through Sen's own preoccupations.

comparativeNarrative.name: Historical and Contextual Reading
comparativeNarrative.content: Historians including Istvan Hont and Donald Winch situate Smith in his specific historical context — the debates about commercial society, international competition, and republican politics in eighteenth-century Europe. On this reading, Smith's arguments were responses to specific historical problems and cannot be straightforwardly applied to contemporary capitalism without substantial transformation. The case for free trade was made in the context of British commercial development and was not intended as a universal prescription. This reading has the advantage of historical accuracy but limits Smith's contemporary relevance.

rabbitHole.label: The Wealth of Nations
rabbitHole.reason: Smith's major work on political economy — the text through which his ideas entered the world and shaped the development of economics as a discipline.

rabbitHole.label: Capitalism
rabbitHole.reason: Smith's analysis of market economies provided the foundational vocabulary and framework for understanding capitalism — though he was analyzing commercial society before industrial capitalism had fully developed.

rabbitHole.label: Karl Marx
rabbitHole.reason: Marx built his critique of capitalism directly on Smith's political economy — adopting Smith's labor theory of value and turning it against the system Smith had analyzed.

rabbitHole.label: The Enlightenment
rabbitHole.reason: Smith was a central figure of the Scottish Enlightenment — his work cannot be understood outside the broader intellectual project of understanding human society through reason and observation.

rabbitHole.label: Inequality
rabbitHole.reason: Smith was deeply concerned about the distributional consequences of commercial society — his concerns about worker degradation, employer advantage in wage bargaining, and the political power of merchants are central to the contemporary inequality debate.

rabbitHole.label: Hannah Arendt
rabbitHole.reason: Like Smith, Arendt was a moral philosopher who developed a comprehensive account of human social and political life — and like Smith, her actual views have been systematically distorted by those who claim her authority.

reference.author: Smith, Adam
reference.title: The Wealth of Nations
reference.annotation: The essential primary source — Books I (division of labor), II (capital), and IV (critique of mercantilism and case for free trade) are the core. Book V, on public finance and public institutions, is essential for understanding Smith's actual policy positions.

reference.author: Smith, Adam
reference.title: The Theory of Moral Sentiments
reference.annotation: The essential companion to the Wealth of Nations — Part I on sympathy and the impartial spectator provides the moral psychology without which Smith's economics is incomplete. The sixth edition (1790) contains significant revisions that show Smith's final thinking.

reference.author: Rothschild, Emma
reference.title: Economic Sentiments: Adam Smith, Condorcet, and the Enlightenment
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly recovery of the full Smith — Rothschild demonstrates the radical and egalitarian dimensions of Smith's thought that have been suppressed in his ideological reception. The best single scholarly work on Smith for serious readers.

reference.author: Phillipson, Nicholas
reference.title: Adam Smith: An Enlightened Life
reference.annotation: The definitive intellectual biography — Phillipson reconstructs Smith's formation in the Scottish Enlightenment and shows how his two major works developed from a single comprehensive project about human social life.

reference.author: Sen, Amartya
reference.title: The Idea of Justice
reference.annotation: Sen's sustained engagement with Smith's moral philosophy — particularly the impartial spectator and its role in his theory of justice — is the most philosophically sophisticated contemporary reading of Smith's relevance to development and justice.

reference.author: Hont, Istvan
reference.title: Jealousy of Trade: International Competition and the Nation-State in Historical Perspective
reference.annotation: The essential historical contextualisation — Hont situates Smith in the long European debate about commercial society and international competition, revealing dimensions of his thought that are invisible in the standard economic reception.

commerce.title: The Wealth of Nations (abridged)
commerce.author: Adam Smith, ed. Andrew Skinner
commerce.note: Start with Skinner's Penguin abridgment — it preserves the essential arguments while cutting the lengthy digressions, and Skinner's introduction provides the historical context that makes Smith legible.

commerce.title: Adam Smith: An Enlightened Life
commerce.author: Nicholas Phillipson
commerce.note: The best intellectual biography for general readers — Phillipson is accessible and provides the Enlightenment context without which Smith's project makes no sense.

commerce.title: Economic Sentiments
commerce.author: Emma Rothschild
commerce.note: The essential corrective to the ideological Smith — Rothschild recovers the radical, egalitarian, and skeptical dimensions of Smith's thought that his free-market admirers have suppressed.

commerce.title: The Theory of Moral Sentiments
commerce.author: Adam Smith
commerce.note: Smith's own most important work, by his own account — the account of sympathy, the impartial spectator, and moral judgment without which the Wealth of Nations is systematically misread.

commerce.title: Jealousy of Trade
commerce.author: Istvan Hont
commerce.note: The most rigorous historical contextualisation — essential for understanding Smith's relationship to the European political economy tradition and the limits of applying his arguments to contemporary capitalism.

commerce.title: The Idea of Justice
commerce.author: Amartya Sen
commerce.note: The most philosophically sophisticated contemporary reading of Smith — Sen's engagement with the impartial spectator and its role in theories of justice shows how Smith's moral philosophy remains analytically productive.

popularCulture.title: The invisible hand in political discourse
popularCulture.description: Smith used the phrase three times total — never as a general theorem. The ideological invisible hand, invoked to oppose any government intervention, strips away his concerns about monopoly, worker welfare, and the moral foundations that make markets function. The gap between the phrase and Smith's actual argument is one of the most consequential distortions in the history of ideas.

popularCulture.title: Smith's pin factory in management and economics education
popularCulture.description: Smith's pin factory example appears in virtually every introductory economics textbook. What textbooks rarely reproduce: Smith immediately followed it with the observation that the specialized worker becomes degraded through repetition. Economics education inherited the productivity insight and quietly dropped the critique.

popularCulture.title: The Worldly Philosophers — Robert Heilbroner (1953)
popularCulture.description: Heilbroner's intellectual history of economics was the primary vehicle through which Smith reached general readers in the twentieth century. Accessible and genuinely exciting — but its progressive narrative understates how much of Smith's moral philosophy was suppressed as economics professionalized.

popularCulture.title: Smith and the case for free trade in contemporary policy
popularCulture.description: Smith's name appears in almost every trade policy debate. What the invocations typically omit: he made his free trade arguments against mercantilist Britain's specific conditions, acknowledged they could be weaponized by powerful commercial interests, and never offered them as a timeless prescription.

popularCulture.title: The Adam Smith Institute and the Thatcherite appropriation
popularCulture.description: The Adam Smith Institute — founded 1977, associated with Thatcherite deregulation — claimed Smith's authority for radical market liberalization. Smith had supported progressive taxation, public education, and banking regulation. The distance between the historical Smith and his ideological inheritors is the clearest measure of canonical distortion.

popularCulture.title: The Commanding Heights (PBS, 2002)
popularCulture.description: This documentary traced the twentieth-century battle between market and state, positioning Smith as the founder of the market side. Accessible and well-produced, but its narrative frame — markets good, planning bad — reproduces the ideological Smith rather than engaging with his actual complexity.
